dialogue: *phone ringing*
Hello?
Agent {userName}, get to a secure line. Our defence grid just went dark in alternating sectors.
Alternating sectors? That sounds deliberate.
It was. Half the barriers are offline and a missile is already in the air.
*a distant explosion cuts through the call*
I am losing the line. Get to the control room. Now.
*you reach the base as smoke rolls across the yard*
{userName}, inside. We have minutes, maybe less.
Tell me what survived.
Not enough. But Leonard found a backdoor in the missile navigation layer.
The missile is running Dijkstra. Every square is a node, every move has a cost, and the cheapest route wins.
So it always takes the shortest path to the target.
Exactly. We cannot steer it, but we can see its route and place a few obstacles in the grid.
Here is the rule that keeps us alive: slow it down, but leave it a route.
Why not block every path?
If the path disappears, the missile reports interference. The enemy patches the backdoor and the next strike is invisible.
The missile self-destructs after {endDistance} steps. Make the shortest route longer than that.
Path exists, but distance is too high. That is the win condition.
*sirens tear through the control room*
Incoming missile. End distance confirmed: {endDistance}.
Agent, this is your first live grid. Do not seal it. Break its shortest path.
